我坐在德克萨斯州的一家酒吧，被掩盖的人包围，看着街道上的人们走在街道上，走在生活中正常，与美好而友好的面孔谈话，感觉世界上的东西更加或不那么正常。归因于Covid的病例和死亡，与其他地方一样，急剧下降。
 如果你只注意到媒体恐惧活动，你会发现这种令人困惑。超过两周前，德克萨斯州州长完全扭转了他毁灭性的锁值政策，并废除了他所有的紧急权力，以及对权利和自由的令人震惊的攻击。
 关于那些锁定有一些非常统一的东西。我的酒店房间是在空中挥舞着枪的马匹戴着牛仔队的照片，以及其他面临元素的崎岖个性化的描绘。这是一个漫画，但德克萨斯人拥抱它。然后一个新的病毒发生了 - 好像在德克萨斯州之前从未发生过 - 而新的Zoom课程拿走了相反的路径，而不是自由而纠纷和控制。
 经过2021年3月2日近一年的废话后，总督终于说得足够了，废除了这一切。城镇和城市仍然可以从事与科迪德有关的恶作剧，但至少他们不再从州长办公室那里获得封面。
 那一刻，一位朋友对我说，这将是我们一直在等待的测试。他们说，完全废除限制会导致大规模死亡。会吗？锁定是否真的控制了病毒？我们很快就会发现，他理论化。
 我知道更好。在世界各地审判了锁定病毒或“抑制爆发”（在安东尼Fauci的话）中的“测试”。每一次严重的实证检查都表明答案是否定的。
 美国有许多公开状态的例子通常在管理疾病方面具有比关闭的国家的表现更好。格鲁吉亚已经于2020年4月24日开业。南达科他州从未关闭过。南卡罗来纳州于五月开幕。佛罗里达州于9月结束了所有限制。在每种情况下，压力嚎叫着即将到来的屠宰。是的，每个开放状态在冬季经历了季节性波，但锁定状态也是如此。 
所以它在德克萨斯州。感谢这个Twitter线程，以及一些自己的谷歌，我们有一个很好的档案馆关于如果德克萨斯州打开了会发生什么。
 Gregg Popovich，NBA San Antonio Spurs的主教练说，开幕是“荒谬的”和“无知”。
 CNN引用了一个ICU护士说“我害怕这是什么样子。”
 虚荣博览会通过本标题越过顶峰：“共和党州长庆祝Covid周年纪念日，大胆计划杀死另外500,000名美国人。”
 有不可避免的Fauci博士：“它只是莫名其妙，为什么你现在要拉回来。”
 罗伯特·弗朗西斯“博德”德克萨斯州的奥尔库克透露自己是一个完整的锁定者：这是一个“大错误”，他说。 “很难逃避这一结论，即它也是一种崇拜。”他指责“牺牲我们德克塞纳人的生活......政治收益”的州长。
 詹姆斯·赫伯林，大西洋的医生和作家，在20k人的推文中说：“现在结束预防措施就像进入马拉松比赛的最后一英里，脱掉你的鞋子吃几只热狗。” 
畅销作者库尔特·Eichenwald翻了出来：“Goddamn。德克萨斯州已经有五种变体，已出现：英国，南非，巴西，纽约＆amp;加利福尼亚州。 NY和CA变体可以削弱疫苗效果。现在白痴@gregabbott_tx抛出了州。“他进一步称政府“杀气”。
 流行病学家Whitney Robinson写道：“我觉得真的很伤心。有些人会生病，并死于未来几周的可避免感染的别人。它的低级化。“
 Pundit Bill Kristol（我不知道他是一个锁定者）写道：“GOV.雅培将负责更加避免的Covid住院和死亡，而不是在德克萨斯州德克萨斯州的所有未记录的移民聚集在一起。“
  病毒Guru Michael Osterholm告诉CNN：“我们走进怪物的嘴。我们只是。“
 Joe Biden着名称德克萨斯州决定开放反映的“尼安德特思想”。
 营养师Eric Feigl-ding说，决定让他想要“呕吐如此糟糕。”
 该州民主党主席表示：“雅培正在做什么是非常危险的。这将杀死德州人。我国的传染病专家警告说，即使我们对疫苗接种的进展，我们也不应该把我们的守卫放在下降。雅培不在乎。“ 
其他国家民主党在一封信中表示，该决定是“过早和有害”​​的信。
 CDC的Rochelle Walensky没有碎嘀嗒声我真的很担心报道，更多国家正在推回我们建议保护人们从Covid-19保护人员的确切公共卫生措施。“
 可能有数数百更多的警告，预测和要求，所有这些都表明了绝对确定的，即基本的社会和市场运作是一个可怕的想法。锁定大厅完全是全力的。然而，我们现在看到了两周多的时间（并且可以说是3月2日在3月2日死亡的锁定，当时政府宣布该决定）？
    CDC具有非常有用的工具，允许任何人比较开放的VS封闭状态。结果对于那些认为锁定是控制病毒的方式的人来说是毁灭性的。在此图表中，我们与开放状态格鲁吉亚，佛罗里达州，德克萨斯州和南卡罗来纳州的封闭状态比较马萨诸塞州和加利福尼亚。
  我们可以从这种可视化方面得出什么结论？它表明，锁定对病毒轨迹没有统计学可观察的影响，并产生严重的结果。公开州一般表现得更好，也许不是因为他们是公开的，而是因为人口统计和季节性的原因而言。封闭的国家似乎没有在缓解方面取得任何成就。
 另一方面，锁定摧毁了行业，学校，教堂，自由和生命，使人口和抢劫基本权利人口贬低。所有在任何情况下都是从病毒的安全名称。
   我没有关于德克萨斯州病毒的未来道路的预测。确实整整一年，艾尔一直在小心不要试图脱口这个病毒，这有一个自有的方式，一些可预测和一些神秘的人。经验已经，或者应该有，谦卑地。政治安排似乎没有能力来控制它，更少终于抑制它。相信有可能控制人们以控制病毒在现代前所未有的灾难。 
对所有上述感染和死亡的预测引人注目的是什么不仅仅是他们都错了。这是他们每个人背后的傲慢和信心。经过全年并直接观察“非药物干预”无法管理病原体，专家仍然与他们心爱的锁定，不能或不愿意查看数据并从中学习任何内容。
 锁定的概念源于错误的前提：你可以将人类分开，如笼子里的大鼠，因此控制甚至消除病毒。一年后，我们明确地知道这并不是真的，最好和最明智的流行病学家都知道这一切。基本工人仍然必须工作;他们必须回到家里，许多人在拥挤的生活条件下。锁定不会消除病毒，它们仅将负担转移到工人阶级。
 现在我们可以看到黑色，白色和全彩色的失败，每天出现在我们的屏幕上由CDC提供。有那种摇动亲锁的Pundit类吗？没有那么多。对精英意见的固执及其反对基本自由的偏见是多么惊人的证明。他们可能都会回应格鲁诺马克思的话：“你会相信谁，我或你自己的眼睛？”
note_en:
I’m sitting at a bar in Texas, surrounded by maskless people, looking at folks on the streets walking around like life is normal, talking with nice and friendly faces, feeling like things in the world are more-or-less normal. Cases and deaths attributed to Covid are, like everywhere else,  falling dramatically.
 If you pay attention only to the media fear campaigns, you would find this confusing. More than two weeks ago, the governor of Texas  completely reversed his devastating lockdown policies and repealed all his emergency powers, along with the egregious attacks on rights and liberties.
 There was something very un-Texan about those lockdowns. My hotel room is festooned with pictures of cowboys on horses waving guns in the air, along with other depictions of rugged individualism facing down the elements. It’s a caricature but Texans embrace it. Then a new virus came along – as if that had never happened before in Texas – and the new Zoom class took the opposite path, not freedom but imposition and control.
 After nearly a year of nonsense, on March 2, 2021, the governor finally said enough is enough and  repealed it all. Towns and cities can still engage in Covid-related mischief but at least they are no longer getting cover from the governor’s office.
 At that moment, a friend remarked to me that this would be the test we have been waiting for. A complete repeal of restrictions would lead to mass death, they said. Would it? Did the lockdowns really control the virus? We would soon find out, he theorized.
 I knew better. The “test” of whether and to what extent lockdowns control the virus or “suppress outbreaks” (in Anthony Fauci’s words) has been tried all over the world. Every serious empirical examination has shown that  the answer is no.
 The US has many examples of open states that have generally had better performance in managing the disease than those states that are closed. Georgia already  opened on April 24, 2020. South Dakota never shut down. South Carolina opened in May. Florida  ended all restrictions in September. In every case, the press howled about the coming slaughter that did not happen. Yes, each open state experienced a seasonality wave in winter but so did the lockdown states.
 So it was in Texas. Thanks to  this Twitter thread, and some of my own googling, we have a nice archive of predictions about what would happen if Texas opened.
 Gregg Popovich, head coach of the NBA San Antonio Spurs,  said opening was “ridiculous” and “ignorant.”
 CNN  quoted an ICU nurse saying “I’m scared of what this is going to look like.”
 Vanity Fair went over the top with this  headline: “Republican Governors Celebrate COVID Anniversary With Bold Plan to Kill Another 500,000 Americans.”
 There was the inevitable  Dr. Fauci: “It just is inexplicable why you would want to pull back now.”
 Robert Francis “Beto” O’Rourke of Texas revealed himself to be a full-blown lockdowner: It’s a “big mistake,” he  said. “It’s hard to escape the conclusion that it’s also a cult of death.” He accused the governor of “sacrificing the lives of our fellow Texans … for political gain.”
 James Hamblin, a doctor and writer for the  Atlantic,  said in a Tweet liked by 20K people: “Ending precautions now is like entering the last miles of a marathon and taking off your shoes and eating several hot dogs.”
 Bestselling author Kurt Eichenwald  flipped out: “Goddamn. Texas already has FIVE variants that have turned up: Britain, South Africa, Brazil, New York & CA. The NY and CA variants could weaken vaccine effectiveness. And now idiot @GregAbbott_TX throws open the state.” He further  called the government “murderous.”
 Epidemiologist Whitney Robinson  wrote: “I feel genuinely sad. There are people who are going to get sick and die bc of avoidable infections they get in the next few weeks. It’s demoralizing.”
 Pundit Bill Kristol (I had no idea that he was a lockdowner)  wrote: “Gov. Abbott is going to be responsible for more avoidable COVID hospitalizations and deaths than all the undocumented immigrants coming across the Texas border put together.”
  Virus guru Michael Osterholm  told CNN: “We’re walking into the mouth of the monster. We simply are.”
 Joe Biden famously  said that the Texas decision to open reflected “Neanderthal thinking.”
 Nutritionist Eric Feigl-Ding  said that the decision makes him want to “vomit so bad.”
 The chairman of the state’s Democratic Party  said: “What Abbott is doing is extraordinarily dangerous. This will kill Texans. Our country’s infectious-disease specialists have warned that we should not put our guard down, even as we make progress towards vaccinations. Abbott doesn’t care.”
 Other state Democrats  said in a letter that the decision was “premature and harmful.”
 The CDC’s Rochelle Walensky didn’t  mince words: “Please hear me clearly: At this level of cases with variants spreading, we stand to completely lose the hard-earned ground we have gained. I am really worried about reports that more states are rolling back the exact public health measures we have recommended to protect people from COVID-19.”
 There are probably hundreds more such warnings, predictions, and demands, all stated with absolute certainty that basic social and market functioning is a terrible idea. The lockdown lobby was out in full force. And yet what do we see now more than two weeks out (and arguably the lockdowns died on March 2, when the government announced the decision)?
    The CDC has a very  helpful tool that allows anyone to compare open vs closed states. The results are devastating for those who believe that lockdowns are the way to control a virus. In this chart we compare closed states Massachusetts and California with open states Georgia, Florida, Texas, and South Carolina.
  What can we conclude from such a visualization? It suggests that the lockdowns have had no statistically observable effect on the virus trajectory and resulting severe outcomes. The open states have generally performed better, perhaps not because they are open but simply for reasons of demographics and seasonality. The closed states seem not to have achieved anything in terms of mitigation.
 On the other hand, the lockdowns destroyed industries, schools, churches, liberties and lives, demoralizing the population and robbing people of essential rights. All in the name of safety from a virus that did its work in any case.
   I’m making no predictions about the future path of the virus in Texas. Indeed for a full year, AIER has been careful about not trying to outguess this virus, which has its own ways, some predictable and some mysterious. The experience has, or should have, humbled everyone. Political arrangements seem to have no power to control it, much less finally suppress it. The belief that it was possible to control people in order to control a virus produced a calamity unprecedented in modern times.
 What’s striking about all the above predictions of infections and deaths is not just that they were all wrong. It’s the arrogance and confidence behind each of them. After a full year and directly observing the inability of “nonpharmaceutical interventions” to manage the pathogen, the experts are still wedded to their beloved lockdowns, unable or unwilling to look at the data and learn anything from them.
 The concept of lockdowns stemmed from a faulty premise: that you can separate humans, like rats in cages, and therefore control and even eradicate the virus. After a year, we unequivocally know this not to be true, something that the best and wisest epidemiologists knew all along. Essential workers still must work; they must go home to their families, many in crowded living conditions. Lockdowns do not eliminate the virus, they merely shift the burden onto the working class.
 Now we can see the failure in black, white, and full color, daily appearing on our screens courtesy of the CDC. Has that shaken the pro-lockdown pundit class? Not that much. What an amazing testament to the stubbornness of elite opinion and its bias against basic freedoms. They might all echo the words of Groucho Marx: “Who are you going to believe, me or your own eyes?”

   亚马逊在社交媒体上推出了一个尖锐的公关，以应对工人，政治家和监管机构的压力海洋。
 为什么这是一个重要的：它的一个风险举措，可以帮助打击短期战斗，但也有可能在公司中拥有荆棘的人和团体建立对抗的敌人模式和群体。
 驾驶新闻：亚马逊靠近一个在阿拉巴马仓库的联盟组织努力的决赛，由于周一结束，邮寄投票。
 根据一位亲盟网站，该公司对联盟进行了积极的反对联盟，包括直接向工人直接发送的短信。据报道，它还从公司目录中删除了数十万级下级仓库工人。 
该公司确认它已聘请当地警方聘请当地警方作为阿拉巴马仓库的安全，亚马逊表示，亚马逊认为是保护往返工作的员工。该公司拒绝评论更广泛的公关活动。
 他们＆＃39;重新说：高管在上周批评了伊丽莎白沃伦和伯尼·桑德斯，直接瞄准伊丽莎白沃伦和伯尼·桑德斯的政治袭击，这是公司并未支付足够的税收或充分补偿员工。
 ＆＃34;我欢迎@sensanders到伯明翰并欣赏他推动进步的工作场所。我经常说我们是雇主的伯尼桑德斯，但这并不是那么正确，因为我们实际提供了一个进步的工作场所，＆＃34;亚马逊消费者老板Dave Clark周三推文。
 更广泛地，亚马逊挑战了这是一个苛刻的工作场所。在推文中到代表。Mark Pocan（D-WIS。），该公司否认了艰难的时间表迫使工人在瓶子中排尿的指控而不是乘坐卫生间休息 - 提示更多的报告确实发生了这一点。
 大局：亚马逊几乎肯定是在未来几年的显微镜下。
 从反垄断角度来看，亚马逊已经占据了在线零售，是Web服务的主要参与者，是在线广告和物理店（整个食物，亚马逊书籍，亚马逊Go）的成长球员。
 该公司长期以来一直批评它支付的税款。仍然，它难以看看亚马逊通过诱饵参议员获得了什么，亚马逊推迟后加倍。 
在线之间：一些亚马逊＆＃39; S的Snappy Retort来自Clark＆＃39; Sit Personal账户，但大多数来自公司亚马逊新闻账户，没有附加名称。  虽然一些看到克拉克作为企业推动背后的声音，但Vox周日报告说，在创始人杰夫·贝佐斯敦促高管敦促批评者的主管前来增加了侵略。  我们的思想泡泡：贝佐斯可能喜欢艰难的谈话，公司可以希望它＆＃39;在自己和集会的舆论上站起来。 但它可以很容易地反馈，对抗关键选区和深化亚马逊＆＃39;识别作为霸道的欺负者。  什么＆＃39;下一篇：联盟投票的结果将于周二开始估计，虽然该过程由国家劳工关系委员会监督，可能需要几天。

   Amazon has launched a sharp-elbowed PR offensive on social media in response to a sea of pressure from workers, politicians and regulators.
 Why it matters: It's a risky move that could help fight short-term battles but also risks establishing a pattern of antagonism against people and groups that could be thorns in the company's side for years to come.
 Driving the news: Amazon is nearing the finale of a union-organizing effort at an Alabama warehouse, with mail-in voting  due to end Monday.
 The company has campaigned aggressively against the union, including a crescendo of  text messages sent directly to workers, according to one pro-union site. It has also  reportedly deleted hundreds of thousands of lower-level warehouse workers from a corporate directory.
 The company confirmed to Axios that it has hired local police to work off-hours as security for the Alabama warehouse, which Amazon said is to protect employees coming to and from work. The company declined to comment on the broader PR campaign.
 What they're saying: Executives have replied to political attacks with pugnacious tweets, directly  targeting Elizabeth Warren and  Bernie Sanders in comments last week amid criticisms that the company doesn't pay enough taxes or adequately compensate employees.
 "I welcome  @SenSanders to Birmingham and appreciate his push for a progressive workplace. I often say we are the Bernie Sanders of employers, but that’s not quite right because we actually deliver a progressive workplace," Amazon consumer boss Dave Clark tweeted on Wednesday.
 More broadly, Amazon has challenged the narrative that it is a harsh place to work. In a tweet to Rep. Mark Pocan (D-Wis.), the company denied  allegations that tough schedules force workers to urinate in bottles rather than take bathroom breaks — prompting more reports that this  is indeed taking place.
 The big picture: Amazon is almost certain to be under a microscope in the coming years.
 From an antitrust perspective, Amazon already dominates online retail and is a major player in Web services and is a growing player in both  online advertising and physical stores (Whole Foods, Amazon Books, Amazon Go).
 The company has long been criticized for the amount of taxes it pays. Still, it's hard to see what Amazon has to gain by baiting senators, and Amazon doubled down after Warren pushed back.
 Between the lines: Some of Amazon's snappy retorts came from Clark's personal account, but most came from the corporate Amazon News account, which has no name attached.
 And while some saw Clark as the voice behind the corporate push, Vox  reported Sunday that the increased aggression came after founder Jeff Bezos urged executives to tackle critics head on.
 Our thought bubble: Bezos may like the tough talk, and the company may hope it's standing up for itself and rallying public opinion. But it could easily backfire, antagonizing key constituencies and deepening Amazon's identification as an overbearing bully.
 What's next: The results of the union vote will begin being counted on Tuesday, though the process, overseen by the National Labor Relations Board, could take a couple of days.
title: 亚马逊的烧焦 - 地球公关战略

我致力于尽快辞去FSF官员，董事和投票成员，并确保FSF的使命的使命和遵守信托要求的持续性。

 虽然这一陈述与＃34带来了;促进[自由软件]的开发和使用，＆＃34;它立即进入Stallman-esque杂草，隐含声明，GNU Toolkit是整个＆＃34;操作系统。＆＃34;从那里，它进入＆＃34;反对＆＃34的竞选活动;感知软件自由的敌人，而不是为此自由而竞选。
 下一节，＆＃34;我们的核心工作，＆＃34;完全从促销活动，我们＆＃39; LL总结了每个段落的一个子弹点：
  我们怀疑密切审查FSF＆＃39;自己的使命陈述 - 而不是简单地假设其任务 - 答案有关RMS＆＃39的许多问题;返回。 FSF将自己描述为一个更加关注的组织，维护历史的一部分，它持有亲爱的，并攻击其感知的敌人，无论是真实的还是不是在自由软件中发现，外展和指导到新的面孔。
note_en:
Last week, Richard M. Stallman—father of the GNU Public  License that underpins Linux and a significant part of the user-facing software that initially accompanied the Linux kernel—returned to the board of the  Free Software Foundation after a two-year hiatus due to his own highly controversial  remarks about his perception of Jeffrey Epstein's victims as "entirely willing." As a result of RMS' reinstatement, Red Hat—the Raleigh, North Carolina-based open source software giant that produces Red Hat Enterprise Linux—has publicly  withdrawn funding and support from the Free Software Foundation:
 Red Hat was appalled to learn that [Stallman] had rejoined the FSF board of directors. As a result, we are immediately suspending all Red Hat funding of the FSF and any FSF-hosted events.
 Red Hat's relatively brief statement goes on to acknowledge an FSF  statement on board governance that appeared on the same day:
 We will adopt a transparent, formal process for identifying candidates and appointing new board members who are wise, capable, and committed to the FSF's mission. We will establish ways for our supporters to contribute to the discussion.
 We will require all existing board members to go through this process as soon as possible, in stages, to decide which of them remain on the board.
 We will add a staff representative to the board of directors. The FSF staff will elect that person.
 The directors will consult with legal counsel about changes to the organization's by-laws to implement these changes. We have set ourselves a deadline of thirty days for making these changes.
 But Red Hat says the statement gives it "no reason to believe that [the statement] signals any meaningful commitment to positive change."
 I am announcing my resignation from the FSF board. (Effective end of Thursday, for administrative reasons.) It's a decision that has been a long time coming for me, but still a hard one: I think the work of the FSF is important, and broken things are the most important to fix.
— Kat Walsh (@mindspillage)  March 25, 2021
 This sentiment seems to be widely shared by many, including at least one FSF board member—Kat Walsh—who  opposed RMS' reinstatement and  resigned her board position on the same day as the board's statement and Red Hat's withdrawal.
 Immediately following Walsh's resignation, the FSF  announced the creation of a new board seat, to be filled with someone from FSF union staff; on Sunday, it  filled this new seat with senior system administrator Ian Kelling.
     The board and voting members look forward to having the participation of the staff via this designated seat in our future deliberations. This is an important step in the FSF's effort to recognize and support new leadership, to connect that leadership to the community, to improve transparency and accountability, and to build trust. There is still considerable work to be done, and that work will continue.
 Knauth, who  began serving in his current role as FSF president in August 2020, declared that it's only a temporary gig:
 I commit myself to resign as an FSF officer, director, and voting member as soon as there is a clear path for new leadership assuring continuity of the FSF’s mission and compliance with fiduciary requirements.
 The elephant in the room that the FSF's remaining board members seem determined to ignore is the continued presence of Stallman himself—who, along with the rest of the FSF board, will soon need to undergo its new "transparent, formal process for identifying [members] who are wise, capable, and committed to the FSF's mission."
  It's probably worth re-examining the FSF's stated  mission to understand its choice to reinstate Stallman, who has been widely panned as far too controversial to make an effective software evangelist.
 The Free Software Foundation is working to secure freedom for computer users by promoting the development and use of free (as in freedom) software and documentation—particularly the  GNU operating system—and by  campaigning against threats to computer user freedom like Digital Restrictions Management (DRM) and software patents.
 Although this statement leads with "promoting development and use of [free software]," it immediately veers off into the Stallman-esque weeds with an implicit declaration that the GNU toolkit is an entire "operating system." From there, it moves into "campaigning against" perceived enemies of software freedom rather than campaigning for that freedom itself.
 The next section, "Our Core Work," moves on from promotion entirely, which we'll summarize here with one bullet point per paragraph:
  We suspect that closely examining the FSF's own mission statements—as opposed to simply assuming its mission—answers many of the questions about RMS' return. The FSF describes itself as an organization far more concerned with maintaining a part of history it holds dear—and attacking its perceived enemies, whether real or not—than with discovering, outreach, and mentorship to new faces in free software.
title: Red Hat在Stallman的回归后从自由软件基础中退出

note:
由于ARS Technica首次将比特币介绍给读者作为“世界上第一个虚拟货币......由神秘的，自由的黑客设计的，并且目前被怪杰地下使用的读者来购买和销售从服务器到手机干扰的所有东西。“十年后，比特币和其他加密货币实际上是主流，甚至最常好的非技术人员都知道区块链基础知识为分散的金融革命（如果您喜欢的持续泡沫）提供电力的基础知识。
 什么比特币是2011年，NFTS为2021年。所谓的“非娱乐令牌”最近几周有一点时间，吸引了追踪数字货物的风险投资现金和潜水投资价值的浪涌。尽管大多数公众都几乎不了解这种基于区块链的数字身份验证系统的工作原理，或者为什么它后面的人为一个GIF支付了6900万美元的人。
  也许开始思考NFT的最简单方法是作为现实世界艺术和收藏品市场中普遍的“真实性证书”的数字版本。然而，NFTS使用加密智能合同和分布式区块链（这些天最常用于以Ethereum顶部的纸张）来证明谁拥有每个独特，正宗的令牌。
 与加密货币一样，这些合同由矿工的集体分布式工作核实，他们将整个系统诚实地遵守其计算工作（这是造成许多讨厌的碳排放的电力）。就像加密货币一样，那些NFT可以在任何数量的市场上直接销售和交易，没有任何集中控制结构，要求这些转移的规则。
 与磨机跑道的NFT不同的是每个令牌的独特性。通过像比特币这样的加密货币，每个单位单位都无法从另一个单元中无法区分，具有相同的值。每个单独的比特币可以像任何其他比特币一样交易或分开（即比特币是可替代的）。 NFT是“非娱乐”的意思是每个人表示具有不同值的不同实体，其不能被分成更小的单元。
 正如任何人都可以开始打印自己的真实证明书（或者任何人都可以启动自己的加密货币来试图“下一个比特币”），任何只有一点技术知识的人都可以开始融入自己的独特的NFT。 Etherscan目前列出了超过9,600个独特的NFT合同，每个人都有自己的信任网络代表和跟踪自己的数字货物。
  这些NFT合同可以代表几乎可以数字上存在的东西：网页，GIF，视频剪辑，您将其命名为。数字艺术家正在使用NFT来创建其作品的“稀缺”验证版本，而可收藏的公司正在使用它们来创建可追溯的不可措施的数字交易卡。视频游戏项目和字符也可以表示为NFT，允许简单地证明所有权和可移植性，即使在不同公司控制的游戏之间（虽然这些游戏的市场仍然非常不成熟）。 
那里有很多甚至的例子。 VID是一个类似的社交媒体网络，提供用户追溯到其发布的视频的所有权（以及与其同样的版税支付）。 Ethereum Name服务正在使用NFT来设置ICANN控制域名服务的分散版本，以查找在线内容。 Aavegotchi是一种奇怪的混合动力车，使用数字宠物代表您在一个名为AAVE的分散金融协议中的股份。基本上，有数百家公司在需要追踪和验证独特数字货物的所有权的情况下，有数百家公司。
    这个想法一直在迅速赶上，至少在投机者中有很多钱来扔掉。数百个不同NFT的非冗长数据库已在上周追踪近40,000名NFT交易的销售额超过4800万美元。最令人驰得是最受欢迎的NFT市场之一，本月早些时候认为其日益增长的每日交易量为190万美元，从前一天两倍增加了相同的数字。代表10,000个独特的像素化的头孢忒嗦的NFT早期的Cryptopunks已经在2017年创造以来，总数超过176,000多美元（上周超过10％）。
  在技​​术水平上，大多数NFT都是基于ERC-721标准的。该框架设置了基本加密系统，以跟踪每个单独的令牌的所有权（通过将其链接到用户控制的数字钱包），并允许在区块链上进行安全验证的传输。
 一些NFT合同已经建立了附加属性和功能，最重要的是。例如，CrypTokitty的NFT包含代表数字化身的独特外观和特征的元数据。元数据还建立了频率如何“培育”新的加密NFT以及将其传递到后代的特征。这些属性在区块链上设置并验证，无论使用加密库的方式或位置，都无法更改。
 然而，当NFT的用来表示数字文件（如GIFS或视频）时，这些文件通常不会在令牌本身中直接存储“在链中”。对于任何体面的文件来做，这可能会得到昂贵的，因为在链中每个用户跨越这些文件的成本。相反，大多数NFTS将实际内容存储为它们元数据中的简单URI字符串，指向数字事物实际驻留的Internet地址。
 将一个分散的分布式数字商品链接到由实际人或公司控制的集中式服务器上托管的内容似乎是奇数。鉴于在几年后绝大多数网页链接变得错失，指向普通的Web地址的NFT似乎不会成为一个很好的长期价值储备。
  许多NFT通过使用蓬勃发展的基于BlockChain的文件网络（如IPFS或Pixelchain）来解决这个问题。这些网络旨在让用户找到，复制和存储可以在任何数量的独立节点中分发的加密签名文件（包括由NFT所有者控制的）。从理论上讲，将NFT链接到IPFS地址可以确保在永久性的情况下将继续访问问题的数字文件，只要有人在IPFS网络中的某些节点上镜像了可验证的副本即可。 
就像真实性证书一样，NFT的值（以及所代表的“独特的”数字项目）与其出处密切相关。例如，对于代表原来的NYAN CAT MEME的NFT花费了56万美元的人显然没有用彩虹小径在它背后购买了一个POP-TART CAT的着名动画GIF的每副本。您仍然可以用几次点击下载自己的相同副本。 NFT甚至没有包括纽亚猫的版权，这至少将使所有者对工作进行一些法律控制（尽管一些NFTS尝试在合同中嵌入这样的权利）。
 是什么让Nyan Cat NFT有趣（和潜在有价值）是由Chris Torres验证和销售，由Chris Torres，于2011年创建并将原始Nyan Cat Video发布到Youtube的人。这为纽亚猫副本提供了独特的历史除非托雷斯从Minting More造成更多的副本，否则无法由任何其他副本（或任何其他NFT，除非托雷斯开始稀释该值）的绑定。在NFT背后的区块链技术可确保GIF版本的保管链可以追溯到托雷斯＆＃39;原创薄荷，无论它有多少次＆＃39; s出售或转移。
     这一事实独自一人真的给出这个nft，而不是比互联网上的所有其他相同的纽亚猫gifs更多的价值？这是一个高度投机的市场来弄清楚。但就像票据杰作的冲程副本一样没有与唯一一个原创的价值相同，来自MEME创建者的验证的“原始”纽亚特猫可以保留给收藏家的一些持续价值。
 只要因为数字货物更容易复制而不是绘画，也不会使比其他更有价值。这是制作摄影印刷品的近乎完美副本，但原始照片仍然可以向右边买家销售数百万美元。
 另一方面，这些NFT可能最终更加类似于那些索赔该文件给您夜空中一颗星的“所有权”的新颖契约。虽然可能对拥有明星的想法可能存在一些感情价值，但没有任何真正的强大市场，其中最令人垂涎​​的星星贸易为大笔资金。就像有很多竞争的组织这些天提供“明星契约”，那么有很多竞争公司可以用自己的NFT产品稀释市场。
  所有这一切都意味着追踪任何给定的NFT的出处都可以对其隐含价值的重要性。 NFT Marketplace SuperRare确保其NFTS仅通过仅为一组“手工采摘艺术家”的铸造代币是“真实的”。同时，NBA顶级拍摄依赖于其NBA许可证，以确保其随机包篮球视频剪辑是每个独特的，并向他们提供“官方”空气。
 但是，特定NFT的原始所有权有很多情况更为值得怀疑。 Game Developer Jason Rohrer本月早些时候尝试出售NFT令牌，以便出售由其他艺术家为2012年游戏教义的其他艺术家创造的艺术品。这并没有取悦许多没有意识到他们的数字工作的艺术家正在转售为代币，至少可以说。 
然后有令牌化的推文，一个简单的服务，可以创建一个畅销的NFT令牌代表服务的任何推文，包括由其他人创建的那些。该服务最近停止了授权推文，包括视觉媒体，如果您的版权保护艺术/摄影被服务授予，则让艺术家使灭亡请求。但这似乎是一个漂亮的吝啬的借助，似乎似乎与欺诈潜力普遍存在。
  还有大理石卡等灰色区域，可以创建一个NFT“框架”，旨在解决特定的唯一网页网址。这使每个帧类似于独特的交易卡，其中包含网页的图片。虽然服务状态清楚地说：“没有在任何区块链中声明或保存或保存第三方内容”，与有关网页的直接链接和隐性关联可能会导致一些棘手的所有权问题。
 随着数千家公司跳进了NFT空间，有一种金色的匆忙心态，似乎骄傲地产生了大量的骗局。甚至合法的NFT努力甚至可以看到他们的价值很快就会迅速消失，如果市场的注意力达到不同的区块链作为“真实”值的商店。 Cryptokitties是2017年底的第一个受欢迎的NFT收藏品之一，2018年的交易卷率为98％，因为缺乏新颖性，缺乏一些更具投机者的球员。
 回到2011年，如果比特币将是持久的金融工具或闪存的技术时尚，则目前尚不清楚。在2021年，您可以对NFTS的未来说同样的事情。
  所以nft给你......没有？它没有与其中的版权所有权的联系，它指的是和＃39;如果也没有阻止创建多个NFT的作品。似乎真的很毫无意义。惊讶地看到那里愿意为任何一个人支付实际钱的人愿意。
note_en:
It has been nearly 10 years now since Ars Technica  first described Bitcoin to readers as “the world’s first virtual currency… designed by an enigmatic, freedom-loving hacker, and currently used by the geek underground to buy and sell everything from servers to cellphone jammers.” A decade later, Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies are practically mainstream, and even most non-techies know  the blockchain basics powering a decentralized financial revolution (or  a persistent bubble, if you prefer).
 What Bitcoin was to 2011, NFTs are to 2021. So-called “non-fungible tokens” are having a bit of a moment in recent weeks, attracting  a surge of venture capital cash and  eye-watering speculative values for traceable digital goods. This despite the fact that most of the general public barely understands how this blockchain-based system of digital authentication works, or why it’s behind  people paying $69 million for a single GIF.
  Perhaps the simplest way to start thinking about NFTs is as a digital version of the various “certificates of authenticity” that are  prevalent in the market for real-world art and collectibles. Instead of a slip of paper, though, NFTs use cryptographic smart contracts and a distributed blockchain (most often built on top of  Ethereum these days) to certify who owns each distinct, authentic token.
 As with cryptocurrencies, those contracts are verified by the collective distributed work of miners who keep the entire system honest with their computational work (the electricity for which creates  a lot of nasty carbon emissions). And just like cryptocurrencies, those NFTs can be sold and traded directly on any number of marketplaces without any centralized control structure dictating the rules of those transfers.
 What makes NFTs different from your run-of-the-mill cryptocurrency is each token’s distinctiveness. With a cryptocurrency like Bitcoin, each individual unit is indistinguishable from another and has an identical value. Each individual Bitcoin can be traded or divided up just like any other Bitcoin (i.e. the Bitcoins are fungible). NFTs being “non-fungible” means each one represents a distinct entity with a distinct value that can’t be divided into smaller units.
 Just as anyone can start printing their own line of Certificates of Authenticity (or anyone can  start up their own cryptocurrency to try to be “the next Bitcoin”), anyone with just a little technical knowhow can  start minting their own distinct NFTs. Etherscan currently  lists over 9,600 distinct NFT contracts, each its own network of trust representing and tracking its own set of digital goods.
  These NFT contracts can represent pretty much anything that can exist digitally: a webpage, a GIF, a video clip, you name it. Digital artists are using NFTs to create “scarce” verified versions of their pieces, while collectible companies are using them to create traceable, unforgeable digital trading cards. Video game items and characters can be represented as NFTs, too, allowing for easy proof of ownership and portability even between games controlled by different companies (though the market for such games is still very immature).
 There are plenty of even odder examples out there.  Vid is a TikTok-like social media network that gives users NFT-traced ownership of their posted videos (and royalty payments for the same). The  Ethereum Name Service is using NFTs to set up a decentralized version of the ICANN-controlled Domain Name Service for finding online content.  Aavegotchi is a weird hybrid that uses digital pets to represent your stake in a decentralized finance protocol called Aave. Essentially, there are hundreds of companies looking to NFTs for situations where they need to trace and verify ownership of distinct digital goods.
    The idea has been catching on quickly, at least among speculators with a lot of money to throw around.  Nonfungibles’ database of hundreds of different NFTs has tracked over $48 million in sales across nearly 40,000 NFT transactions in just the last week. Rarible, one of the most popular NFT marketplaces,  saw its daily trading volume hit $1.9 million earlier this month, tripling the same number from just a day before.  Cryptopunks, an early NFT representing 10,000 unique pixellated avatars, has seen over $176 million in total transactions since its creation in 2017 (with over 10 percent of that volume coming in the last week).
  On a technical level, most NFTs are built on  the ERC-721 standard. That framework sets up the basic cryptographic system to track ownership of each individual token (by linking it to user-controlled digital wallets) and allow for secure, verified transfer on the blockchain.
 Some NFT contracts have built additional attributes and features on top of that standard. The NFT for a  cryptokitty, for instance, contains metadata representing that digital avatar’s unique look and traits. That metadata also establishes rules for how often it can “breed” new cryptokitty NFTs and what traits it will pass down to future generations. Those attributes are set and verified on the blockchain, and they can’t be altered no matter how or where the cryptokitty is used.
 When NFT’s are used to represent digital files (like GIFs or videos), however, those files usually aren’t stored directly “on-chain” in the token itself. Doing so for any decently sized file could get prohibitively expensive, given the cost of replicating those files across every user on the chain. Instead, most NFTs store the actual content as a simple URI string in their metadata, pointing to an Internet address where the digital thing actually resides.
 It may seem odd to link a system of decentralized, distributed digital goods to content hosted on centralized servers controlled by actual people or companies. Given that the vast majority of webpage links  become defunct after just a few years, an NFT pointing to a plain-old web address wouldn’t seem to be a good long-term store of value.
  Many NFTs get around this by using burgeoning blockchain-based file networks such as  IPFS or  pixelchain. These networks are designed to let users find, copy, and store cryptographically signed files that could be distributed among any number of independent nodes (including ones controlled by the NFT owner). In theory, linking an NFT to an IPFS address could ensure the digital file in question will continue to be accessible in perpetuity, as long as  someone has mirrored a verifiable copy on some node in the IPFS network.
  Just like a certificate of authenticity, the value of an NFT (and the “unique” digital item it represents) is strongly tied to its provenance. The person who  spent $560,000 for an NFT representing the original Nyan Cat meme, for instance, obviously didn’t purchase every copy of the famous animated GIF of a pop-tart cat with a rainbow trail behind it. You can still download your own identical copy  with a few clicks. The NFT doesn’t even include the  copyright to Nyan Cat, which would at least give the owner some legal control over the work (though some NFTs try to embed such rights in their contracts).
 What makes the Nyan Cat NFT interesting (and potentially valuable) is that it was verified and sold by Chris Torres, the person who created and posted  the original Nyan Cat video to YouTube in 2011. That gives  this copy of Nyan Cat a unique history and a tie to the meme’s creation that can’t be matched by any other copy (or any other NFT, unless Torres starts diluting the value by minting more). And the blockchain technology behind the NFT ensures the chain of custody for that version of the GIF can be traced back to Torres' original minting, no matter how many times it's sold or transferred.
     Does that fact alone really give this NFT any more value than all of the other identical Nyan Cat GIFs floating around on the Internet? That’s for a highly speculative market to figure out. But just  as a stroke-for-stroke copy of a Vermeer masterpiece doesn’t have the same value as the one-of-a-kind original, a verified “original” Nyan Cat from the meme’s creator may retain some persistent value to collectors.
 Just because digital goods are easier to copy than paintings doesn’t make one less valuable than the other, either. It’s trivial to make a near-perfect copy of a photographic print, but original photographs can still  sell for millions of dollars to the right buyer.
 On the other hand, these NFTs might end up being more akin to  those novelty deeds that claim the document gives you “ownership” of a star in the night sky. While there’s probably some sentimental value to the idea of owning a star, there isn’t any real robust market where the most coveted stars trade for large sums. And just like there are a lot of competing organizations offering “star deeds” these days, there are a lot of competing firms that could dilute the market with their own NFT offerings.
  All of this means that tracing the provenance of any given NFT can be of prime importance to its implicit value. NFT marketplace  SuperRare ensures its NFTs are “authentic” by only minting tokens for a set of “hand-picked artists” for the time being.  NBA Top Shot, meanwhile, relies on its NBA license to make sure its randomized packs of basketball video clips are each unique and have an “official” air to them.
 But there are plenty of situations where the original ownership of a particular NFT is more questionable. Game developer Jason Rohrer drew some controversy earlier this month by  trying to sell NFT tokens for artwork originally created by other artists for his 2012 game  The Castle Doctrine. This did not please many of the artists who were not aware their digital work was being resold as a token, to say the least.
 Then there’s Tokenized Tweets, a simple service that can create a sellable NFT token representing any tweet on the service,  including ones created by other people. The service has recently stopped tokenizing tweets that include visual media, and it lets artists make takedown requests if their copyrighted art/photography is tokenized by the service. But that seems like a pretty skimpy Band-Aid for an offering that seems rife with fraud potential.
  There are also gray areas like  Marble Cards, which lets you create an NFT “frame” intended to go around a specific, unique webpage URL. That makes each frame akin to a unique trading card with a picture of a webpage on it. While the service states clearly that “no third-party content is claimed or saved on any blockchain,” the direct link and implicit association with the webpage in question could lead to some thorny questions of ownership.
 With literally thousands of companies jumping into the NFT space, there’s a gold rush mentality that seems primed to spawn plenty of scams. And even legitimate NFT efforts could see their values fade away quickly if the market’s attention moves on to a different blockchain as its store of “authentic” value. Cryptokitties, one of the first popular NFT collectibles in late 2017,  saw transaction volume plummet 98 percent in 2018 as high Ethereum fees and lack of novelty drove some of the more speculative players away.
 Back in 2011, it was unclear if Bitcoin was going to be a lasting financial instrument or a flash-in-the-pan technological fad. And here in 2021, you can say the same thing about the future for NFTs.
  So the NFT gives you... nothing? It has no connection to copyright ownership of the work it refers to and there's nothing stopping the creation of multiple NFTs for the same work either.  Seems really pointless. Surprised to see there's people willing to pay actual money for any of this.
title: ARS Technica的NFTS的非可租借指南
title_en: Ars Technica’s non-fungible guide to NFTs

当Apple首次宣布计划在IOS 14中引入IOS选择的选择选择，整个广告行业都遭到震惊。
 Facebook留下没有石头推迟苹果已经推迟的软件更新 - 通过批评全页报纸广告的iPhone制造商或通过释放展示用户的横幅消息来批评为什么个性化广告很好。
 看到Facebook启动对抗Apple的所有攻击并不奇怪。社交媒体国王多年来创造了十亿美元的广告网络，即将盯着巨大的损失。
 然而，看到另一个广告巨头令人惊讶的是，谷歌没有筹集一个关于iOS 14变化的单一眉毛。事实上，它们足以满足应用程序的功能。
 TRUE，Google有自己的操作系统，Android。但仍然，他们的Admob SDK用于App Store上的超过100k应用程序。这是一个非常大的广告收入。
 但现在谷歌终于揭示了自己的计划离开第三方饼干，我们终于知道过去一年酿造的东西。
 3月3日，字母拥有的搜索引擎再次确认其计划在2022年底逐步逐步逐步逐步淘汰第三方跟踪饼干。公告的最具兴趣方面是他们不建立，使用或支持任何替代用户的承诺 - 级别广告跟踪方法。至少不是Chrome，它是迄今为止Web上最占主导地位的浏览器。 
虽然对隐私权过渡的意图肯定是消费者和隐私倡导者的令人耳目一新的变化，但谷歌的决定改变网络跟踪工程如何从数字广告行业的其他球员汲取批评。
 谷歌的大胆移动转向匿名的小组跟踪只描绘了一个玫瑰色的图片，而且还有一些符合眼睛的东西。搜索引擎巨头方便地扫过地毯下的一些东西。证明谷歌最终将从这些变化中获得最大的事情。
  谷歌的隐私第一网实际上是垄断第一网，因为科技巨头是最受欢迎的
 对于初学者来说，谷歌并没有禁止所有cookie。第三方和第一方饼干之间存在差异。
 第一方cookie是在与网站相同的域中创建的，并没有在其他地方共享。它们是有用的，可以改善整体用户体验 - 通过存储分析数据或登录凭据来实现。
 另一方面，第三方cookie是网站上的广告网络嵌入的代码，以跟踪网络上的动作。除了作为跨站点跟踪代理时，第三方cookie帮助广告发布商构建用户配置文件（如您在Netflix纪录片，社交困境中看到的，并为个人用户设置了目标广告活动。
 现在，当谷歌决定拼写第三方cookie的结尾时，它会这样做，因为他们不需要唯一地跟踪您以显示相关的广告和建议。字母所有公司已经拥有各种产品和服务收集的大量第一方用户数据。 
因此，即使谷歌正在朝着更加隐私的解决方案迁移，此举也是为了使搜索引擎王成为最多。与他们不同，小广告商没有足够的第一方数据来向用户提供相关广告。相反，用于依赖于用户浏览活动的广告代理商用于销售广告。
 一旦第三方饼干逐步淘汰，广告商将被迫更依赖于谷歌，这不仅拥有世界上最占主导地位的浏览器，而且还可以通过搜索，YouTube和其他服务继续收集第一方信息。
 实际上，谷歌正试图强迫公司向他们询问数据，而不是让他们自己收集。这意味着广告商仍将获得相同的用户数据级别，而是仅通过向现在支付给Google的保费成本。
 相信谷歌将把它的背部扭转在为客户的缘故中制造了一个技术巨人的事情，这将是愚蠢的。
 隐私第一网络就像一名医生说我不会吸毒你，而他们已经在你身体中注射了血清。
 这也是谷歌手称的用户隐私很重要，所以让我们作为所有在线数据的守门员
 粗鲁地，谷歌的隐私第一网站已经设法杀死了一块石头的两只鸟。他们赢得了消费者的心，并确保他们的竞争对手保持在海湾。 
隐私第一网实际上是谷歌中途移动守门员的案例，并以适合议程的方式定义自己的隐私标准化，并更好地控制数字生态系统。有效地，它是一个伪装的垄断第一网。
  Facebook对Google的隐私第一Web的回复显示目标广告不会随访
 我们已经知道Facebook努力扰乱Apple的匿名广告跟踪功能。 Facebook通过带小名片来抗击iPhone制造商。
 在那里，苹果不采用像谷歌这样的苛刻的做法。相反，Cupertino Tech Giant只是给用户更透明和选择。
 在谷歌的情况下，他们已经证实了第三方饼干的日子被编号。此外，它们确保Chrome不支持其他用户级标识符进行跟踪方法。
 从本质上讲，谷歌不相信给予小广告商的选择。然而与Facebook对Apple的批评相反，他们对谷歌最新变革的回应一直是聋音。
 显而易见的是，社交媒体国王从中丢失很少，而且有很多原因。 
对于一个，值得注意的是，Google逐步淘汰cookie仅限于浏览器，而不是整个Android操作系统。这意味着隐私第一网络不会影响移动广告行业 - 这是Facebook的受众网络从其大部分收入中汲取的。
 此外，Facebook已经从第三方饼干和两年前走向第一方数据。
 然而，Facebook只会从Google的隐私 -  First Web中获得的最大原因是即将到来的隐私沙箱技术不会结束有针对性的广告。相反，它可能只会使个性化广告更有效 - 一个福音为出版商和最终用户的隐私噩梦。
 对于不熟悉的，谷歌的联邦队列学习（Floc）是替代第三方饼干的技术。在絮絮上的帮助下，Google计划根据类似的利益将其受众汇总为群组（或群组）。
 到目前为止他们透露的内容，Google服务器将无法存储您的浏览习惯，通过设备侧（这是您的Chrome浏览器）发生用户分类。
 本质上，絮凝物是谷歌的赌注，让Chrome浏览器做了用户谱构，其中第三方跟踪器可能先做哪些
 因此，Floc不会真正解决Cookie的隐私问题。相反，它将为广告商开辟更好的机会，以基于行为，人口统计学和其他指标向类似人销售物品。 
广告商并不真正关心您的目标，只要他们能够针对客户并确保与他们的产品进行接触 - 这解释了为什么Facebook实际上会从这种变化中获得很多。
  谷歌可能感觉到人们变得更加隐私。难怪，Firefox和Safari浏览器通过他们的隐私保留举措获得更多的市场份额。
 科技巨头不得不在竞标中抵消这一威胁，以加强对广告生态系统的垄断，同时留在武器和＃39;从反信托后果的长度。
 因此，虽然拼盘的引入确保没有人以令人毛骨悚然的方式收集足够的用户数据，但它也可以为数字广告的未来设置一个先例。
 谷歌确保不许多广告网络都有机会以隐私保存方式构建用于挖掘用户数据的工具。他们还在制作传统的用户追踪方法上过时。这是一个明确的迹象表明谷歌试图将其作为广告业的主销巩固的职位。
 “别邪恶”是一句被认为是谷歌的官方座右铭一次。但现在它不再被考虑，人们可以希望他们成为邪恶 - 因为字母所有的公司努力在整个广告行业上巩固其标记。
note_en:
When Apple first announced its plans to introduce opt-in ad-tracking in iOS 14 the whole advertisement industry was left in shock.
 Facebook left no stone unturned in pushing back against Apple’s already postponed software update — be it by criticizing the iPhone maker in full-page newspaper ads or through releasing banners messages that showcased users why personalized ads are good.
 It wasn’t surprising to see Facebook launching an all attack against Apple. The social media king has created a billion-dollar advertisement network over the years and was about to stare at huge losses.
 However, it was astonishing to see the other advertisement giant, Google not raising a single eyebrow with respect to the iOS 14 changes. In fact, they were quick enough to embrace the feature across their apps.
 True, Google has its own operating system, Android. But still, their Admob SDK is used in over 100K apps on the App Store. That’s a pretty big advertisement revenue at stake.
 But now that Google has finally revealed its own plans to move away from third-party cookies, we finally know what had been brewing in the past year.
 On March 3rd, the Alphabet-owned search engine once again confirmed its plans to phase out third-party tracking cookies by the end of 2022. The most intriguing aspect of the announcement was their commitment to not build, use or support any alternative user-level ad tracking methods. At least not on Chrome, which is by far the most dominant browser on the web.
 While the intention to transition towards a privacy-first web is certainly a refreshing change for consumers and privacy advocates yet Google’s decision to alter how web tracking works is certainly drawing criticism from the other players of the digital advertising industry.
 Google’s bold move to shift towards anonymized group tracking only paints a rosy picture and there’s more than what meets the eye. The search engine giant has conveniently swept a few things under the carpet. Things that prove that Google will eventually gain the most from these changes.
  Google’s Privacy First Web Is Actually Monopoly First Web as the Tech Giant Stands To Benefit From It the Most
 For starters, Google isn’t banning all cookies. There’s a difference between third-party and first-party cookies.
 First-party cookies are created on the same domain as the website and aren’t shared elsewhere. They’re useful to improve the overall user experience — be it through storing analytics data or login credentials.
 On the other hand, third-party cookies are pieces of code embedded by advertising networks on websites to track your movements across the web. Besides acting as cross-site tracking agents, third-party cookies help ad publishers build user-profiles(like you’d have seen in the Netflix documentary, Social Dilemma) and set up targeted ad campaigns for individual users.
 Now when Google decides to spell the end of third-party cookies, it’s doing so because they don’t require to track you uniquely anymore to show relevant ads and suggestions. The Alphabet-owned company already has an enormous amount of first-party user data collected through their various products and services.
 So even though Google is moving towards a more privacy-focused solution, the move stands to benefit the search engine king the most. Unlike them, small advertisers don’t possess enough first-party data to serve relevant ads to the user. Instead, ad agencies used to rely on the user browsing activity for selling ads.
 Once the third-party cookies phase-out, advertisers would be compelled to be more dependent on Google, which not only owns the most dominant browser in the world but can also keep collecting first-party information through Search, YouTube, and other services.
 In effect, Google is trying to force companies to ask them for the data rather than letting them gather it on their own. This means advertisers will still get the same level of user data but only by paying a premium cost to Google from now on.
 It’ll be foolish to believe that Google will turn its back on the very thing that made them a tech giant just for the sake of customers.
 Privacy-first web is like a doctor saying I won’t drug you while they’d already injected a serum in your body long back.
 It’s also like Google saying user privacy is important, so let us act as the gatekeeper of all your online data
 Sardonically, Google’s privacy-first web has managed to kill two birds with one stone. They’ve won the hearts of consumers and ensured their competitors are kept at bay.
 Privacy first web is actually a case of Google moving the goalposts midway and defining their own standardization of privacy in a way that suits their agenda and gives better control over the digital ecosystem. Effectively, it’s a monopoly first web in disguise.
  Facebook’s Response to Google’s Privacy First Web Shows Targeted Ads Aren’t Going Anywhere
 We already know of Facebook’s efforts to disrupt Apple’s anonymous ad tracking feature. Facebook protested against the iPhone maker by bringing the small business card.
 There, Apple wasn’t adopting harsh practices like Google just did. Instead, the Cupertino tech giant was only giving users more transparency and a choice.
 In Google’s case, they’ve confirmed that the days of third-party cookies are numbered. Plus, they’re ensuring Chrome won’t support other user-level identifiers methods for tracking.
 In essence, Google doesn’t believe in giving small advertisers a choice. Yet contrary to Facebook’s criticism of Apple, their response to Google’s latest changes has been deaf tone.
 It’s increasingly becoming apparent that the social media king has very little to lose from it and there are quite a few reasons for it.
 For one, it’s worth noting that Google phasing out cookies is only restricted to the browser and not on their Android operating system as a whole yet. This means privacy-first web doesn’t impact the mobile advertising industry — which is where Facebook’s Audience Network draws most of its revenue from.
 Besides, Facebook had already moved away from third-party cookies and towards first-party data more than two years ago.
 Yet the biggest reason why Facebook would only set to gain from Google’s privacy-first web is the fact that upcoming privacy sandbox technology won’t end targeted advertisements. Instead, it might only make personalized ads more effective — a boon for publishers and a privacy nightmare for end-users.
 For the unfamiliar, Google’s  Federated Learning of Cohorts(FLoC) is the technology that’ll replace third-party cookies. With the help of FLoCs, Google plans to aggregate its audiences into groups(or cohorts) based on similar interests.
 From what they’ve revealed so far, Google servers won’t store your browsing habits and the user categorization would happen on the client-side(which is your Chrome browser) through on-device machine learning.
 Essentially, FLoC is Google’s bet to make the Chrome browser do the user profiling which third-party trackers could do earlier
 So, FLoC won’t really solve the privacy concerns of cookies. Instead, it’ll open better opportunities for advertisers to sell items to similar people based on behavior, demography, and other metrics.
 Advertisers don’t really care who you are as long as they can target customers and ensure engagement with their products — which explains why Facebook is actually going to gain a lot from this change.
  Google could sense that people are becoming more privacy-conscious. No wonder, Firefox and Safari browsers were gaining more market share through their privacy-preserving moves.
 The tech giant had to counter that threat in a bid to strengthen its monopoly over the ad ecosystem while staying at arm's length from anti-trust ramifications.
 So, while the introduction of FLoC does ensure that no one can collect enough user data in a creepy manner, it also sets a precedent for the future of digital advertising.
 Google has ensured that not many ad networks would stand a chance to build tools for mining user data in a privacy-preserved way. They’re also making the traditional methods of user tracking on the web obsolete. It’s a clear indication that Google is trying to consolidate its position as the kingpin of the advertisement industry.
 “Don’t be evil” is a phrase that was considered Google’s official motto once. But now that it’s no longer considered, one can expect them to be evil — as the Alphabet-owned company strives to entrench its mark on the entire advertisement industry.
title: 谷歌的杀戮第三方饼干，因为他们更好地占据了网上的计划
title_en: Google's Killing Third-Party Cookies as They've Better Plans to Dominate the Web

我们开始在没有知道原因的情况下见面。我们知道我们有共同的兴趣。我们知道我们觉得在彼此的公司中觉得理解，这是一个很好的开端。我们知道我们的热情不会误读，我们的好奇心的强度是无知的。我们聚集在一起，我们互相努力的项目，我们对其他调整到相同频率的别人留意。起初几十个，然后几百，最终成千上万。我们的利益随着部落扩大而扩大。像能量吸引的人一样，很快就有数百家本地化的聚会在世界各地绽放。虽然生活变化 - 学校结束，责任accrue  - 信号始终存在。致电我们回到了解我们的人的公司，谁在我们所做的同样的事情点亮。我们希望分享我们所学到的内容。我们想了解其他人必须分享的内容。我们想教导和教导并向文化前进。我们想做我们的部门来建立一个自由和开放的未来，尽可能安全，并且作为建立它的部落。这是信号。这就是为什么多年来，里程和任何数量的灾难不会阻止它。
  今年的视觉灵感始于视频游戏。具体而言，Atari 2600盒艺术。我们选择了盒子艺术，因为它代表了进入另一个世界的网关，一个信号标信标被称为互联网将有一天绽放的数字空间。两套图像在盒子上。生动的漫画告诉您游戏意味着描绘，笨拙，胖胖的像素图像，显示出实际的游戏。 “这是”，盒子说。 “当你看到这个时，想象一下'。对于那些想象力看它清楚的人来说，消息变得“将其翻译成那”。一旦某种类型的孩子学习这样的伎俩，就没有回头。
        一个简单的等式踢开了门，你即使你也可以让像素做事。如果你学习密码，可能会更好的事情。掌握管理这个隐藏世界的规则，几乎没有任何东西。不是每个追逐8位幽灵或射击空间入侵者的人成为黑客。但是许多人看到了门，被叫走过它，因为我们的一些人，信号不能被拒绝。
  类似的事情在音乐世界中发生了类似的事情。电子仪器 - 可以模拟真实仪器的采样器，可能听起来像现场鼓套件的鼓机 - 突然向音乐家和爱好者突然出现。价值主张是您可以创建音乐，即使您没有备忘录，或在不聘用四方的情况下为您的作品添加字符串。然而，对于那些具有特殊弯曲的人，这错了这一点。为什么你会浪费这些惊人的机器重新创造你已经知道的声音？对于这些孩子来说，这些电子工具的真正能力是能够听起来只有以前过的任何东西。像视频游戏一样，这些尴尬的盒子覆盖在开关和旋钮中，所有方向落后的电线，打开门。另一方面？嘻哈（音乐。自由式。电器。 Techno。新世界。一旦你看到音乐可以成为你想象的任何东西，就没有路了。信号有你。以下是当数字无政府状态跳跃到音乐主流时的时代的一些样本曲调。
            对于一个适合我们主题的更多音乐，请查看Spotify播放列表，我们正在调用“DC29-The Signal”。
  Tron Disney的深入奇怪，催眠尝试在计算机内带来1982个受众，是最可能存在的主题电影。这些计划是填写的。其中一些人从真正的UNIX命令中获取他们的名字。遍布销毁邋的程序的文字（和令人敬畏的）错误。主角是一种如此迷恋的编程，他在机器里面得到了自己的身体战斗，与已经成为感知和邪恶的失控流程。
  MAX HEADROM MAX HEADROM是一个不可阻挡的信号。创建是一个低veejay，他击中了一个神经并获得英国电视电影来肉体肉体。这部电影产生了一个赛季英国系列和十四集，在美国电视上运行了四个集。这些电视表明，又会出现了书籍，歌曲，漫画，视频游戏，甚至作为助攻头的辐条。这可能已经证明了80年代正在寻找一个蛇子，光滑的机器人最好的朋友。但最大的净空没有我们完成。 1987年11月22日，最大跳跃了气隙。那天晚上，芝加哥的WGN电视的电视通过全球净水室Regalia的海盗劫持了两次。他谈到了WGN的可口可乐，漫画和空中人物，并以奇怪的打屁股段结束了节目。肇事者仍然很大。 Max Headroom现在可以在现实素数中自由漫游，偶尔在电影和说唱视频中发现。 
从这段时间的焦虑造成了很多伟大的科幻小说 - 唠叨的恐惧是你所知道的世界隐藏一个更大的陌生世界，你无法感知。 Philip K. Dick's Valis是一个关于未知世界的召唤的杰作。随着80年代初期所带来的，通过创造性的爆炸，就绪球员们是一个有趣的爆炸。如果您还没有读取神经元库，请不要完成本指南 - 只是读了神经元语。
         更重要的是，有T文件。多年来，免费文本文件是主要信号矢量。有互联网连接的人可以抓住所有最新的黑客新闻，对逆产量的好吃的东西，也许可以抓住NMAP的宣布。一个人可以追随死牛的崇拜，以获得他们的专利混合物和冈卓。很快，一个微妙的门打开了。那些不得不了解更多人的人如何经历它。很多人，也许甚至你。或者激发你的人。信号赢了。永恒的呼叫将我们共同努力工作，以更好的世界，并传播其福音 - 将其他人带来，直到我们所有人都被发现。

note_en:
We begin to meet without knowing exactly why. We know we have common interests. We know that we feel understood in each other’s company, and that’s a good start. We know our enthusiasm won’t be misread, that the intensity of our curiosity is wordlessly accepted. We gather ourselves together, we work on each other’s projects and we keep an eye out for the others tuned to the same frequency.    A few dozen at first, then a few hundred, eventually thousands. Our interests expand as the tribe expands. Like energy attracts like, and soon hundreds of localized gatherings bloom around us all over the world.  And even though life changes - school ends, responsibilities accrue - the signal is always there. Calling us back to the company of the people who understand us, who light up at the same things we do. We want to share what we’ve learned. We want to learn what others have to share. We want to teach and be taught and move the culture forward. We want to do our part to construct a future as free and open, as secure and as resilient as the tribe that’s building it.  That’s the signal. That’s why the years, the miles and any number of calamities don’t stop it.
  This year’s visual inspiration started with video games. Specifically, Atari 2600 box art.  We picked the box art because it represents a gateway into another world, a signal beacon that called a generation into the digital space where the internet would one day bloom.   Two sets of images on the box. Vivid cartoons to tell you what the game meant to portray, and clumsy, fat-pixeled images that showed actual gameplay. ‘This is that’, the boxes said. ‘When you see this, imagine that’. For those with the imagination to see it clearly, the message becomes ‘translate this into that’. Once a certain type of kid learns a trick like that, there is no turning back.
        A simple equation kicks open the door, and you realize even you can make pixels do things. Maybe better things, if you learn the cipher. Master the rules that govern this hidden world, and there is almost nothing you can’t do.  Not everyone who chased 8-bit ghosts or shot Space Invaders became a hacker. But many saw the door and were called to walk through it, because for some of us, the signal can’t be denied.
  Similar things were happening in the world of music.Electronic instruments - samplers that could emulate real instruments, drum machines that could sound like a live drum kit - were suddenly available to musicians and hobbyists alike. The value proposition was that you could create music even if you didn’t have a drummer handy, or add strings to your composition without hiring a quartet.  For those with a particular bent of mind, however, this missed the point. Why would you waste these amazing machines re-creating sounds you already knew? For these kids, the real power of these electronic tools is the ability to sound like nothing at all that has ever come before. Like video games, these awkward boxes covered in switches and knobs, trailing wires in all directions, opened a door. On the other side? Hip-hop. Freestyle. Electro. Techno. The New World. And once you see that music can be anything you imagine, there is no way back. The signal has you.  Here are some sample tunes from the era when that digital anarchy jumped the air gap into the musical mainstream.
            For a bunch more music that fits our theme, check out the Spotify Playlist we’re calling “DC29-The Signal”.
  TRON   Disney’s deeply weird, hypnotic attempt to bring 1982 audiences inside the computer is the most on-theme movie that could possibly exist. The programs are personified. Some of them take their names from real Unix commands.  There are literal (and awesome) bugs running around that destroy sloppy programs. The protagonist is a human so obsessed with programming that he gets himself inside the machine to do physical battle with a runaway process that has become sentient and evil.
  Max Headroom   Max Headroom is the unstoppable signal personified. Created to be a lowly AI veejay, he hits a nerve and gets a UK TV movie to flesh out his backstory. That movie spawns a one-season UK series and a fourteen-episode, two season run on US TV. These TV shows, in turn, spawn books, songs, comics, video games and even a turn as spokes-head for Coca-Cola. That would probably have been proof enough that the 80s were looking for a snarky, slick-talking robot best friend. But Max Headroom wasn’t done with us. On the 22 of November, 1987 Max jumped the air gap.   That night WGN TV in Chicago had its airwaves hijacked twice by a pirate in full Max Headroom regalia. He spoke about Coca-Cola, cartoons and on-air personalities from WGN, and ended the show with a weird spanking segment. The perpetrators remain at large. Max Headroom is now free to roam in reality prime, and is occasionally spotted in movies and rap videos.
  A lot of great science fiction from this time period deals with a particular anxiety - the nagging fear that the world you know is hiding a larger, stranger world you cannot yet perceive. Philip K. Dick’s VALIS is a masterpiece about the call of the unseen world on those who sense its presence. Ready Player One is a fun romp through the creative explosion that accompanied all the new shiny toys that the early 80s brought is. And if you haven’t read Neuromancer, don’t finish this guide - just read Neuromancer.
         And more importantly, there are T-files. For years free textfiles were a primary signal vector. Anybody with an internet connection could grab the latest Phrack for all the latest hacker news, counterculture goodies and maybe catch the announcement of nmap. One could follow the Cult of the Dead Cow online for their patented blend of tech and gonzo. Soon enough, a subtle door opened. Those who had to know more figured out how to go through it. Many did, maybe even you. Or the person who inspired you. The signal wins out. The eternal call brings us together to work on a better world, and to spread its gospel - to bring in the others, until all of us are found.
